gojoseon-founding.title: Founding of Gojoseon
gojoseon-founding.summary: Dangun Wanggeom set his capital at Asadal and founded Gojoseon. Built on the principle of Hongik Ingan ("to broadly benefit humankind"), it was the first nation of the Korean people, and Gojoseon's history extended over some 2,000 years.

wiman-joseon.title: Rise of Wiman (Founding of Wiman Joseon)
wiman-joseon.summary: Wiman, an exile from the Chinese state of Yan, took refuge in Gojoseon, then ousted King Jun and seized the throne. Wiman Joseon expanded its power on the foundation of iron-age culture and dominated northern Korea and parts of Manchuria.

gojoseon-fall.title: Fall of Gojoseon
gojoseon-fall.summary: After more than a year of campaigns, Han Wudi's forces captured Wanggeomseong and ended Gojoseon. The Han then established Four Commanderies, taking control of the northern peninsula.

silla-founding.title: Founding of Silla
silla-founding.summary: Bak Hyeokgeose, said to have hatched from a sun-bright egg, was raised to the throne — the founding of Silla. Of the three kingdoms, Silla survived the longest and ultimately unified the peninsula.

goguryeo-founding.title: Founding of Goguryeo
goguryeo-founding.summary: Jumong (King Dongmyeong) escaped Buyeo and founded Goguryeo at Jolbon. Goguryeo grew into a great power across Manchuria and the northern peninsula and lasted some 700 years.

baekje-founding.title: Founding of Baekje
baekje-founding.summary: Onjo founded Baekje at Wiryeseong (modern Seoul area). A son of Jumong, he split with his brother Biryu and built the kingdom in the Han River basin.

gaya-founding.title: Founding of Gaya
gaya-founding.summary: Six chiefs in Garakguk welcomed Suro from a golden box and founded the Gaya confederacy. Backed by abundant iron production, Gaya traded across the southern peninsula and into Japan, sustaining a distinctive culture.

baekje-pyongyang-attack.title: Baekje's Attack on Pyeongyang under King Geunchogo
baekje-pyongyang-attack.summary: Under King Geunchogo, Baekje attacked Pyeongyang and killed Goguryeo's King Gogugwon. The campaign marked Baekje's mid-fourth-century zenith and gave Baekje the upper hand in the early Three Kingdoms order.

gwanggaeto-conquests.title: Conquests of King Gwanggaeto
gwanggaeto-conquests.summary: King Gwanggaeto ascended at 18 and waged campaigns against 19 states. In 22 years he won 64 fortresses and 1,400 villages, building Goguryeo into the greatest power in Northeast Asia. The achievements are recorded on the Gwanggaeto Stele.

gwanggaeto-silla-rescue.title: Goguryeo Rescues Silla
gwanggaeto-silla-rescue.summary: Goguryeo dispatched 50,000 cavalry and infantry to repulse a joint Wa attack on Silla. Recorded on the Gwanggaeto Stele, the campaign expanded Goguryeo's influence into the south.

jangsu-pyongyang-capital.title: King Jangsu Moves the Capital to Pyongyang
jangsu-pyongyang-capital.summary: King Jangsu moved Goguryeo's capital from Gungnaeseong (modern Ji'an) to Pyongyang and intensified the southern policy. The move signaled a shift from northward expansion to consolidation south, sharpening conflict with Baekje and Silla.

hanseong-fall.title: Fall of Baekje's Capital Hanseong
hanseong-fall.summary: King Jangsu of Goguryeo led 30,000 troops, captured Hanseong, and killed King Gaero of Baekje. Baekje moved its capital to Ungjin (Gongju) to rebuild, and Goguryeo seized the Han River basin.

silla-buddhism.title: Silla Adopts Buddhism
silla-buddhism.summary: Following the martyrdom of Ichadon, King Beopheung made Buddhism the official faith of Silla. Buddhism became the ideology of royal authority and national unity, and laid the foundation for Silla's cultural flowering.

salsu-battle.title: Battle of Salsu
salsu-battle.summary: General Eulji Mundeok of Goguryeo annihilated 300,000 invading Sui troops at the Salsu (modern Cheongcheon) River. The battle ended Sui's invasion of Goguryeo and is remembered as one of the great victories in Korean military history.

ansi-battle.title: Battle of Ansiseong
ansi-battle.summary: The garrison and people of Ansiseong, led by their commander, repelled a massive Tang army under Emperor Taizong over an 88-day siege. The victory ended Tang's invasion and is celebrated alongside Salsu as a representative defensive triumph — though the Ansi commander's full name has not been transmitted.

baekje-fall.title: Fall of Baekje
baekje-fall.summary: Allied forces of Silla and Tang invaded Baekje. At Hwangsanbeol, General Gyebaek's 5,000-man final stand fought a Silla army of 50,000. Gyebaek fell in battle, and Baekje's 678 years ended. A revival movement followed but failed.

silla-unification.title: Silla's Unification of the Three Kingdoms
silla-unification.summary: Allied with Tang, Silla destroyed Baekje and then Goguryeo, unifying the Three Kingdoms. Yet when Tang sought to control the peninsula directly, Silla had to fight the Silla–Tang War to drive Tang out.

nadag-war-victory.title: Victory in the Silla–Tang War
nadag-war-victory.summary: After unification, Silla repelled Tang's attempt to occupy the peninsula and secured genuine independence. Silla thereby fixed its territory south of the Daedong and Wonsan line and opened the Unified Silla era.

balhae-founding.title: Founding of Balhae
balhae-founding.summary: Dae Joyeong founded Balhae at Mount Dongmo, leading Goguryeo refugees and Mohe forces. Balhae claimed succession from Goguryeo and grew into a great power spanning Manchuria and the Maritime Province.

bulguksa-seokguram.title: Construction of Bulguksa and Seokguram
bulguksa-seokguram.summary: At the height of Silla under King Gyeongdeok, Prime Minister Kim Daeseong oversaw the construction of Bulguksa and Seokguram. These masterpieces of Silla Buddhist art still stand in Gyeongju and are inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage list.

jangbogo-cheonghaejin.title: Jang Bogo Establishes Cheonghaejin
jangbogo-cheonghaejin.summary: Jang Bogo set up Cheonghaejin on Wando, suppressed pirates, and dominated East Asian maritime trade. He built a network linking Silla, Tang, and Japan, earning the title "King of the Sea" — a high point of peninsular maritime activity.

goryeo-founding.title: Founding of Goryeo
goryeo-founding.summary: Wang Geon founded Goryeo and unified the Later Three Kingdoms. He absorbed local strongmen through marriage diplomacy and a northern policy, completing unification in 936 and inheriting the Goguryeo legacy under the name "Goryeo."

balhae-fall.title: Fall of Balhae
balhae-fall.summary: Balhae fell to a Khitan (Liao) attack in only some fifteen days. Many royals and aristocrats fled to Goryeo, and Crown Prince Dae Gwang-hyeon led a group accepted there — formalizing Goryeo's claim of Balhae's heritage.

khitan-1st-invasion-seohui.title: First Khitan Invasion — Seo Hui's Diplomatic Negotiation
khitan-1st-invasion-seohui.summary: The Khitan invaded with 800,000 troops, but Seo Hui's diplomacy turned them back and won the Six Garrisons east of the Yalu. Goryeo extended its territory north and the negotiation has come down as a model in Korean diplomatic history.

goryeo-tripitaka.title: Carving of the Tripitaka Koreana
goryeo-tripitaka.summary: During Mongol invasions, Goryeo undertook the carving of the Tripitaka Koreana to invoke the Buddha's protection. Some 80,000 wooden blocks form the most complete extant Buddhist canon and are preserved at Haeinsa today.

gwiju-battle.title: Battle of Gwiju
gwiju-battle.summary: General Gang Gamchan crushed a third major Khitan invasion at Gwiju (modern Gusong) in the climactic battle of the Goryeo–Khitan wars. Of 100,000 Khitan troops, only some 1,500 returned home, sealing Goryeo's victory.

mushin-coup.title: Military Coup of 1170
mushin-coup.summary: Jeong Jung-bu, Yi Ui-bang and other military officers, resentful of civilian discrimination, staged a coup. The civil aristocratic order centered on the literati was dismantled, beginning roughly a century of military rule.

mongol-1st-invasion.title: First Mongol Invasion
mongol-1st-invasion.summary: Sartai's Mongol army invaded Goryeo and devastated the country. General Bak Seo's defense of Guju Fortress was a notable success, but in the end Goryeo was forced to seek peace and accept heavy demands.

ganghwa-island-move.title: Move to Ganghwa Island (Choe Military Regime)
ganghwa-island-move.summary: The Choe regime under Choe U moved the capital to Ganghwa Island to fight the Mongols and waged a 28-year resistance. The protracted war battered the people, and the Choe regime was eventually overthrown — leading to peace.

metal-type.title: World's First Metal Movable-Type Printing
metal-type.summary: Goryeo printed books with metal movable type — some 200 years before Gutenberg's metal type (c. 1450). It demonstrates a leading edge in printing technology.

jikji-printing.title: Printing of Jikji Simche Yojeol
jikji-printing.summary: *Jikji Simche Yojeol* was printed at Heungdeoksa Temple in Cheongju with metal movable type. The original text has been lost, but the surviving printed copy — the world's oldest extant metal-type print — is held at the Bibliothèque nationale de France.

joseon-founding.title: Founding of Joseon
joseon-founding.summary: Yi Seong-gye seized power in late Goryeo through the Wihwa-do Retreat (1388) and founded Joseon. Built on Confucian ideology, Joseon ruled the peninsula for some 500 years and shaped its civilization profoundly.

hanyang-capital-move.title: Move of the Capital to Hanyang
hanyang-capital-move.summary: Taejo Yi Seong-gye moved the capital from Gaegyeong of Goryeo to Hanyang (modern Seoul). Hanyang was chosen by geomantic principles, and together with Gyeongbokgung, it became the heart of Joseon — a capital lasting more than 600 years.

angbuilgu.title: Invention of the Angbu Sundial and Jagyeongnu
angbuilgu.summary: Jang Yeong-sil under King Sejong invented the Angbu sundial and the self-striking Jagyeongnu water clock. These were great advances in Joseon science and were placed in public spaces so that ordinary people could read the time.

hangul-creation.title: Creation of Hunminjeongeum
hangul-creation.summary: King Sejong personally devised the Korean alphabet (Hunminjeongeum) for the people. With its ingenious phonetic principles, Hangeul let illiterate commoners read and write with ease.

hangul-promulgation.title: Promulgation of Hunminjeongeum
hangul-promulgation.summary: King Sejong proclaimed Hangeul through the publication of the *Hunminjeongeum Haerye* (Explanations and Examples). The work is inscribed in UNESCO's Memory of the World; October 9 is celebrated today as Hangeul Day.

gyeongguk-daejeon.title: Completion of the Gyeongguk Daejeon
gyeongguk-daejeon.summary: Compiled across the early Joseon period, the *Gyeongguk Daejeon* code was completed and promulgated under King Seongjong. Spanning the State Council to the Six Ministries — *Yi*, *Ho*, *Ye*, *Byeong*, *Hyeong*, *Gong* — it codified Joseon governance.

imjin-war.title: Outbreak of the Imjin War
imjin-war.summary: Under Toyotomi Hideyoshi, Japan invaded Joseon with 200,000 troops. Hanseong fell in twenty days, and within two months the country was on the brink of collapse — but Admiral Yi Sun-sin's naval victories and the campaigns of righteous armies turned the tide.

hansan-battle.title: Battle of Hansan
hansan-battle.summary: Off Hansan Island near Tongyeong, Yi Sun-sin used the "crane-wing" formation to annihilate the Japanese fleet. Naval supremacy passed to Joseon, changing the course of the war; the engagement is regarded as Yi Sun-sin's representative victory.

haengju-battle.title: Battle of Haengju
haengju-battle.summary: General Gwon Yul, with fewer than 3,000 troops at Haengju Sanseong, drove off a 30,000-strong Japanese force. The story of women carrying stones in their aprons (*haengjuchima*) to support the soldiers is legendary; Haengju is one of the three great victories of the Imjin War.

jeongyujairan.title: Outbreak of the Jeongyu Reinvasion
jeongyujairan.summary: After failed peace talks, Japan invaded Joseon again, beginning the Jeongyu Reinvasion. Early on, Won Gyun's defeat at Chilcheollyang nearly destroyed the Joseon navy, but Yi Sun-sin was reinstated as Three-Province Naval Commander and rebuilt it.

myeongryang.title: Battle of Myeongnyang
myeongryang.summary: Yi Sun-sin used the Myeongnyang Strait's tidal currents to defeat a Japanese fleet of about 133 ships with only 13. The miraculous victory in the face of overwhelming odds is rated unprecedented in world naval history.

noryang-battle.title: Battle of Noryang — End of the Imjin War
noryang-battle.summary: Admiral Yi Sun-sin pursued the retreating Japanese fleet at Noryang and won, but fell to a stray bullet during the final fighting. The victory closed seven years of war, and Yi is remembered as one of history's greatest commanders.

jeongmyo-horan.title: Jeongmyo Manchu Invasion
jeongmyo-horan.summary: After Injo's coup brought a pro-Ming policy, Later Jin (later Qing) invaded Joseon. Joseon proposed peace and was forced into a brotherhood treaty, accepting an unequal relationship with Later Jin.

byeongja-horan.title: Byeongja Manchu Invasion — Humiliation at Samjeondo
byeongja-horan.summary: Emperor Hong Taiji of Qing invaded with 120,000 troops. King Injo held out at Namhansanseong for 45 days but ultimately surrendered. At Samjeondo he performed the rite of three kneelings and nine prostrations to the Qing emperor — a symbol of national humiliation.

hong-gyeongnae-revolt.title: Hong Gyeongnae Rebellion
hong-gyeongnae-revolt.summary: Resenting discrimination against the Pyeongan region and political corruption in late Joseon, Hong Gyeongnae led peasants, miners, and merchants in revolt. They held the area north of the Cheongcheon River for a time before being suppressed in five months — a notable late-Joseon popular uprising.

imsul-uprising.title: Imsul Peasant Uprising
imsul-uprising.summary: Some 70 uprisings broke out in Jinju and across the southern provinces in protest against extortion. Resistance to the "three administrations" — land, military service, and grain loans — was central, exposing structural weaknesses and triggering reform discussions.

donghak-uprising.title: Donghak Peasant Movement
donghak-uprising.summary: Led by Jeon Bongjun, the Donghak peasants rose to expel corrupt officials, drive out foreigners, and reform the political order. Connecting to the Gabo Reform, the movement triggered Japanese and Chinese intervention and became a cause of the First Sino-Japanese War.

gabo-reform.title: Gabo Reform
gabo-reform.summary: Under Japanese pressure but driven also by domestic reformers, sweeping institutional reforms were enacted. Modern measures such as abolishing the civil-service exam, ending hereditary status, and emancipating slaves were introduced — a major impact on social change.

daehan-empire.title: Founding of the Korean Empire
daehan-empire.summary: Gojong proclaimed himself emperor and established the Korean Empire, pursuing autonomous modernization. Performing the imperial rite at the Hwangudan altar and adopting the era name "Gwangmu," he declared independence from the Sino-centric order and joined the modern East Asian state system.

eulsa-treaty.title: Eulsa Treaty (Loss of Diplomatic Sovereignty)
eulsa-treaty.summary: After winning the Russo-Japanese War, Japan coerced the conclusion of the Eulsa Treaty, stripping Korea of its diplomatic sovereignty. Although the emperor refused to ratify it, the treaty effectively turned Korea into a Japanese protectorate; resistance, including patriot Min Yeong-hwan's suicide, followed.

hague-envoys.title: Hague Envoys — Gojong's Forced Abdication
hague-envoys.summary: To expose the unjust treaty to the world, Gojong sent Yi Jun, Yi Sang-seol, and Yi Wi-jong to the Hague Peace Conference. Japanese pressure denied them entry; using the mission as a pretext, Japan forced Gojong to abdicate.

army-dissolution-uibyeong.title: Disbandment of the Army — Spread of Righteous Armies
army-dissolution-uibyeong.summary: Under the Treaty of 1907 (the Jeongmi Seven-Article Treaty), Japan dissolved the Korean army. Disbanded soldiers joined the righteous armies, and resistance spread across the country — the wellspring of later armed independence forces from the March 1st Movement onward.

ahn-junggeun-action.title: Ahn Jung-geun's Action at Harbin
ahn-junggeun-action.summary: At Harbin, Ahn Jung-geun shot Itō Hirobumi, the architect of Japanese aggression. He framed his act as the killing of a leader in the East Asian war, asserted his standing as a prisoner of war rather than a criminal, and is regarded today as one of the greatest figures in independence history.

annexation.title: Korea–Japan Annexation
annexation.summary: Japan ended Korea's sovereignty under coercion and concluded the Annexation Treaty with the Empire. Thus the Korean Empire fell, and 35 years of colonial rule began.

march-first.title: March 1st Movement
march-first.summary: The reading of the Declaration of Independence by 33 national representatives sparked a nationwide rising. More than two million people participated, and Yu Gwan-sun led the rally at the Aunae market in her hometown. The movement deepened global awareness of Korean independence and led to the founding of the Provisional Government.

provisional-government.title: Founding of the Provisional Government of the Republic of Korea
provisional-government.summary: The Provisional Government was founded in Shanghai as the central body of the independence movement. Adopting a republic in form, it carried on the cause for 27 years; figures like Kim Gu, An Chang-ho, and Yi Dong-hwi led its work.

bongodong-cheongsalli.title: Battles of Bongo-dong and Cheongsanri
bongodong-cheongsalli.summary: General Hong Beom-do at Bongo-dong and General Kim Jwa-jin at Cheongsanri inflicted major defeats on Japanese forces. Both were among the greatest victories of independence-army units against the Japanese, raising the prospect of independence by armed force.

lee-bongchang-yoon-bonggil.title: The Acts of Yi Bong-chang and Yun Bong-gil
lee-bongchang-yoon-bonggil.summary: Yi Bong-chang threw a grenade at the Japanese emperor in Tokyo, and Yun Bong-gil hurled a bomb at the Japanese commander at Hongkou Park, Shanghai. Their actions made the Provisional Government's existence known internationally and helped secure support from the Chinese government.

gwangbokgun-founding.title: Founding of the Korean Liberation Army
gwangbokgun-founding.summary: The Provisional Government founded the Korean Liberation Army as its regular force. Cooperating with allied forces in the war against Japan, the army planned a domestic offensive in 1945 — but liberation came first.

liberation.title: Liberation
liberation.summary: Japan's defeat in the Pacific War restored Korea's sovereignty after 35 years. On August 15, 1945, the country celebrated its liberation, the day still observed as Liberation Day.

division.title: Division of the Peninsula (38th Parallel)
division.summary: By U.S.–Soviet agreement, the peninsula was divided along the 38th parallel. As the U.S. and the Soviet Union occupied south and north respectively and set up separate governments, division became an entrenched reality.

jeju-4-3.title: Jeju 4·3 Incident
jeju-4-3.summary: Resistance on Jeju Island against South-only elections led to an uprising; the suppression by military and police led to mass civilian killings. Long obscured by official prohibition, the case was officially apologized for by the state in 2003 and continues to be addressed.

rok-founding.title: Founding of the Republic of Korea
rok-founding.summary: Following the May 10 election, the Republic of Korea was officially founded with Syngman Rhee as its first president — Asia's first democratic republic. Soon after, however, a separate government was established in the north, and division was structured into being.

dprk-founding.title: Founding of the Democratic People's Republic of Korea
dprk-founding.summary: Kim Il-sung proclaimed the establishment of the DPRK in the north. Founded under Soviet support as a socialist state, it stood opposed to South Korea, completing the division of the peninsula.

korean-war-start.title: Outbreak of the Korean War
korean-war-start.summary: On June 25, 1950, North Korean forces launched a surprise attack across the 38th parallel, beginning the Korean War. The Republic of Korea was unprepared and forced into rapid retreat; the international community was thrown into shock.

seoul-fall-1950.title: Fall of Seoul (Three Days into the Invasion)
seoul-fall-1950.summary: Within three days of the invasion, North Korean forces had occupied Seoul. The Han River bridge was blown to slow the advance, leaving refugees and soldiers stranded amid chaos; the government withdrew to Busan and prepared to keep fighting.

incheon-landing.title: Incheon Landing
incheon-landing.summary: Under General Douglas MacArthur, the UN forces' Incheon Landing succeeded. Coming ashore on September 15 and recapturing Seoul, the UN forces broke through the Pusan Perimeter and advanced north — the turning point of the war.

china-intervention-1-4-retreat.title: Chinese Intervention — January 4 Retreat
china-intervention-1-4-retreat.summary: As UN forces neared the Yalu River, Chinese armies intervened in force, pushing them back. Seoul fell again on January 4, 1951, when ROK and UN forces retreated under Chinese pressure — and millions of Seoul citizens once again fled south of the Han.

armistice.title: Signing of the Armistice
armistice.summary: Three years of war stopped with an armistice, not a peace treaty, and the state of war legally continues. The principal signatories were North Korea, China, and the UN forces; the Republic of Korea did not sign — the foundation of present-day tensions on the peninsula.

april-revolution.title: April 19 Revolution
april-revolution.summary: Students and citizens, outraged by the rigged March 15 election, rose up and toppled the Syngman Rhee regime. Faced with violent suppression, students and citizens nationwide protested; Rhee resigned — a milestone in Korean democracy.

military-coup-1961.title: May 16 Military Coup
military-coup-1961.summary: Park Chung-hee led a military coup that toppled the Chang Myon government and seized power. The Supreme Council for National Reconstruction held sway, and Park was later inaugurated president, ruling for 18 years.

economic-miracle.title: Miracle on the Han (Economic Development)
economic-miracle.summary: An export-led growth strategy under President Park Chung-hee delivered rapid economic expansion. Per-capita income, only about $100 in the 1960s, rose dramatically in the 1970s, earning the moniker "Miracle on the Han."

gwangju-uprising.title: May 18 Gwangju Democratization Movement
gwangju-uprising.summary: Gwangju citizens rose for democratization in resistance to Chun Doo-hwan's New Military and the expansion of martial law. Brutal suppression by the army caused many deaths, and the spirit of the movement became the wellspring of subsequent democratization.

democracy-1987.title: June Democratic Uprising
democracy-1987.summary: Park Jong-chul's death from torture and the April 13 declaration to maintain the constitution provoked nationwide protests demanding direct presidential elections. The June 29 Democratization Declaration won the constitutional revision and is the watershed of Korean democratization.

seoul-olympics.title: Seoul Olympics
seoul-olympics.summary: The 24th Summer Olympic Games were held in Seoul, presenting the Republic of Korea to the world. With 160 nations participating, the Games marked Korea's economic growth and democratization on the world stage and greatly enhanced its international image.

korea-japan-worldcup.title: World Cup Semifinal Run
korea-japan-worldcup.summary: At the 2002 FIFA World Cup co-hosted by Korea and Japan, the Korean national team reached the semifinals — a historic feat. Under coach Guus Hiddink, Korea took down major opponents including Poland, the United States, Portugal, Italy, and Spain.

hallyu-kpop-global.title: Global Spread of K-pop and the Korean Wave
hallyu-kpop-global.summary: K-pop artists such as BTS and BLACKPINK, the Academy Award–winning film *Parasite* (2020), and more carried Korean culture worldwide. The Korean Wave (Hallyu) — across music, film, drama, food, and beauty — has taken its place as a global cultural phenomenon.
